items.prompt_md: In `is_same_tree`, what happens the moment one node is `None` and its counterpart in the other tree is not?
options: The function returns `False` immediately, without recursing further
The `None` is treated as a wildcard that matches any node
The recursion skips that pair and continues comparing the remaining children
The function raises an exception
items.answer: The function returns `False` immediately, without recursing further
items.explanation_md: One side having a node where the other has `None` is a shape mismatch — the trees can't be the same, so the check short-circuits to `False` right there instead of comparing anything else.

items.prompt_md: Why does comparing two trees require recursing on BOTH trees at once, rather than traversing one tree and looking up matches in the other?
options: Because structural equality depends on the exact shape at every position, and recursing in lockstep is what lets shape mismatches (missing or extra nodes) be caught immediately, at the position where they occur
Because Python cannot hold two tree references in the same function call
Because a single traversal is always faster, so lockstep recursion is only used for style
Because trees cannot be traversed more than once each
items.answer: Because structural equality depends on the exact shape at every position, and recursing in lockstep is what lets shape mismatches (missing or extra nodes) be caught immediately, at the position where they occur
items.explanation_md: Comparing corresponding node pairs directly means a missing node, an extra node, or a differing value is caught exactly where it happens — a single traversal with a lookup elsewhere can't line up positions the same way.

items.prompt_md: What are the three conditions that must ALL hold for two nodes `p` and `q` to be considered 'matching' in a same-tree check?
options: Both are None, OR both are non-None with equal values AND their left subtrees match AND their right subtrees match
Both have the same depth, and both are leaves
Both have equal values, regardless of their children
Both are non-None, and at least one of their children matches
items.answer: Both are None, OR both are non-None with equal values AND their left subtrees match AND their right subtrees match
items.explanation_md: The base case is both sides being None (an empty match); otherwise both must be present, hold equal values, and have recursively matching left AND right subtrees — missing any one of these breaks the match.

items.prompt_md: Why doesn't flattening each tree into a pre-order list of values, then comparing the two lists, correctly decide whether two trees are the same shape?
options: A pre-order value sequence alone doesn't uniquely determine shape unless null markers are also encoded — without them, different shapes can produce the same flattened sequence
Pre-order traversal visits nodes in a different order every time it's run
Lists in Python cannot store integers and None values together
Comparing two lists is always slower than comparing two trees directly
items.answer: A pre-order value sequence alone doesn't uniquely determine shape unless null markers are also encoded — without them, different shapes can produce the same flattened sequence
items.explanation_md: Without recording where the `None`s fall, two structurally different trees can flatten to the identical value sequence, so a shape check needs the null markers baked in — at which point it's just the node-by-node comparison in disguise, plus an extra pass.

items.prompt_md: What does it mean for the same-tree check's `and` to 'short-circuit', and why does that matter for performance?
options: The moment any node pair fails to match, the function returns False immediately without visiting the rest of either tree, avoiding wasted comparisons
It means the recursion always visits every node in both trees before returning any result
It means the two subtree checks run in parallel threads
It means value comparisons are skipped whenever both nodes are None
items.answer: The moment any node pair fails to match, the function returns False immediately without visiting the rest of either tree, avoiding wasted comparisons
items.explanation_md: As soon as one node pair fails to match, there's no need to check the remaining subtrees — the whole comparison is already False, so `and` skips that work.

items.prompt_md: How does checking whether a tree is 'symmetric' (a mirror image of itself) build on the same-tree comparison idea?
options: It recursively compares the tree's left and right subtrees to each other, but mirrored — checking node A's left against node B's right, and vice versa, instead of left-against-left
It runs the standard same-tree check comparing the tree against a copy of itself, left-against-left and right-against-right
It only needs to check that the root's two children have equal values
It requires flattening the tree into a sorted array first
items.answer: It recursively compares the tree's left and right subtrees to each other, but mirrored — checking node A's left against node B's right, and vice versa, instead of left-against-left
items.explanation_md: Symmetry means the left half is a mirror of the right half, so the recursive matcher must cross-compare: left child of one side against right child of the other, at every level.

items.prompt_md: For 'is this smaller tree a subtree of the bigger tree,' how does the same-tree check get reused?
options: The same-tree check is tried rooted at every node of the bigger tree, stopping as soon as one of them matches
The same-tree check is only ever tried once, rooted at the bigger tree's root
The smaller tree's values are hashed and looked up in a hash set of the bigger tree's values
The bigger tree is flattened into a sorted list and binary-searched for the smaller tree's root value
items.answer: The same-tree check is tried rooted at every node of the bigger tree, stopping as soon as one of them matches
items.explanation_md: Subtree-matching walks the bigger tree and, at each of its nodes, asks 'does the same-tree check pass here?' — reusing the exact same-tree matcher, just anchored at different starting points.

items.prompt_md: What's a common bug when writing a recursive tree-comparison function like `is_same_tree`?
options: Checking `p.val == q.val` before confirming both `p` and `q` are non-None, causing an attribute error when one is None
Recursing into the left children before the right children
Returning True instead of False when both nodes are None
Using recursion instead of an explicit stack
items.answer: Checking `p.val == q.val` before confirming both `p` and `q` are non-None, causing an attribute error when one is None
items.explanation_md: Accessing `.val` on a `None` node raises an error, so the None-checks (both None, or exactly one None) must come before any comparison that touches `.val`.

items.prompt_md: For a 'root-to-leaf path sum' style question on a single tree, how does it relate to the comparison/structural-recursion pattern in this lesson?
options: It's a related but distinct recursion shape — it accumulates a running value down a single tree's paths, rather than matching node pairs across two trees
It's identical to same-tree, just applied to one tree compared against itself
It requires comparing every leaf to every other leaf pairwise
It cannot be solved recursively and needs an explicit stack
items.answer: It's a related but distinct recursion shape — it accumulates a running value down a single tree's paths, rather than matching node pairs across two trees
items.explanation_md: Path-sum problems thread a running total down one tree's recursion; same-tree/subtree problems instead walk two trees in lockstep comparing pairs — both are recursive tree shapes, but they carry different state for different purposes.

items.prompt_md: In the worst case, where the two trees really are identical and nothing short-circuits early, what is the time complexity of `is_same_tree` on two trees with n and m nodes?
options: O(min(n, m)) — every node pair is visited once until one tree runs out of nodes
O(n * m) — every node in one tree is compared against every node in the other
O(n + m) time but O(n * m) space
O(log(n * m))
items.answer: O(min(n, m)) — every node pair is visited once until one tree runs out of nodes
items.explanation_md: The recursion walks both trees in lockstep, one node pair at a time, and stops as soon as either tree runs out of nodes or a mismatch is found — so it never does more than O(min(n, m)) comparisons.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of `is_same_tree`, and what determines it?
options: O(min(h_p, h_q)) — bounded by the shallower tree's recursion depth, since the recursion stops as soon as one side runs out
O(n + m), because both trees must be fully copied into new lists before comparison
O(1), because no extra memory is ever used beyond the two input trees
O(n * m), because a comparison matrix between the two trees must be stored
items.answer: O(min(h_p, h_q)) — bounded by the shallower tree's recursion depth, since the recursion stops as soon as one side runs out
items.explanation_md: The recursion's call stack depth tracks how far down both trees it has gone together, so it's bounded by the shorter of the two trees' heights, not the total node counts.

items.prompt_md: 'Is tree B a subtree of tree A' calls the same-tree check at every node of A. What's the worst-case time complexity of this naive approach, with n nodes in A and m nodes in B?
options: O(n * m) — for each of A's n nodes, a same-tree check costs up to O(m) in the worst case
O(n + m) — each tree is visited only once in total
O(n log m) — a binary-search-like check is done at each node
O(m) — only tree B's nodes ever need to be visited
items.answer: O(n * m) — for each of A's n nodes, a same-tree check costs up to O(m) in the worst case
items.explanation_md: In the naive version, every one of A's n nodes triggers a fresh same-tree attempt that can cost up to O(m) before failing, giving O(n * m) overall in the worst case (e.g., deeply skewed trees with many partial matches).

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether two binary trees are structurally identical, with matching values at every position. Which approach fits?
options: Recurse on both trees in lockstep: match if values agree and both pairs of children recursively match
BFS one tree, collect its values into a set, then BFS the other and check set membership
Sort each tree's values into an array and compare the two sorted arrays
Two pointers walking each tree's leftmost and rightmost paths
items.answer: Recurse on both trees in lockstep: match if values agree and both pairs of children recursively match
items.explanation_md: Structural equality needs both value AND shape to match at every position, which a set of values or a sorted list would lose. Recursing on both trees together, comparing node pairs, preserves the shape information the check depends on.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether a binary tree is a mirror image of itself (symmetric around its center). What approach fits?
options: Recurse comparing the left and right subtrees cross-wise (left's left against right's right, left's right against right's left)
Flatten the tree with an in-order traversal and check if the resulting list is a palindrome
BFS by level and check each level's node count is even
Hash every subtree and compare hash counts
items.answer: Recurse comparing the left and right subtrees cross-wise (left's left against right's right, left's right against right's left)
items.explanation_md: Symmetry is a mirrored structural match, so the recursive comparator needs to cross-compare children rather than compare each side to itself — a variant of the same-tree recursion shape.

items.prompt_md: You're given a large tree and a small candidate tree, and asked whether the small tree appears, unchanged, rooted at some node within the large tree. What's the best approach?
options: Walk the large tree, and at every node, try the same-tree check rooted there against the small tree
BFS the large tree collecting a set of all node values, then check the small tree's root value is in that set
Sort both trees' values and check one sorted list is a contiguous slice of the other
Compare only the two trees' root values
items.answer: Walk the large tree, and at every node, try the same-tree check rooted there against the small tree
items.explanation_md: 'Appears unchanged somewhere inside' means trying the exact same-tree comparison at every possible anchor point in the bigger tree, not just checking membership of values.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether a binary tree is height-balanced (the height difference between any node's left and right subtrees is at most 1, for every node). What's the best approach?
options: Bottom-up DFS that returns each subtree's height, short-circuiting to a sentinel value the instant an imbalance is found anywhere below
Compare the tree against a mirrored copy of itself using the same-tree recursion
BFS level by level, counting how many nodes are on each level
In-order traversal, checking the resulting list is sorted
items.answer: Bottom-up DFS that returns each subtree's height, short-circuiting to a sentinel value the instant an imbalance is found anywhere below
items.explanation_md: Balance is a bottom-up aggregate (subtree heights combining upward), computed with a single DFS pass rather than a two-tree comparison — still a tree-recursion problem, just a different shape from same-tree.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine if a root-to-leaf path exists in a binary tree whose node values sum to a target. What's the best approach?
options: DFS down each path, carrying the running sum so far, checking at each leaf whether it equals the target
Recurse comparing this tree against a second copy of itself node by node
BFS level by level, summing each level's values independently
Flatten to a sorted array and use two pointers to find a pair summing to the target
items.answer: DFS down each path, carrying the running sum so far, checking at each leaf whether it equals the target
items.explanation_md: A root-to-leaf sum is a single-tree, top-down path aggregate — DFS threading the running total down the recursion, distinct from a two-tree same-tree comparison but still a tree-recursion approach.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether two given strings are anagrams of each other (same letters, same counts, any order). What's the best approach?
options: Build a hash map of letter counts for each string and compare the two maps
Recurse on both strings in lockstep like a same-tree check, comparing characters position by position
BFS over the characters treating each string as a tree
Two pointers converging from each string's ends, since anagram order doesn't matter
items.answer: Build a hash map of letter counts for each string and compare the two maps
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: there's no tree structure here — anagram checking is a frequency-counting problem over flat sequences, so a hash map of counts is the right tool, not tree recursion.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether a singly linked list is a palindrome (reads the same forwards and backwards). What's the best approach?
options: Find the middle with slow/fast pointers, reverse the second half, then walk both halves comparing values
Recurse on the two halves in lockstep exactly like a same-tree comparison
Compare the list against a mirrored copy using cross-wise recursion, as with tree symmetry
Try matching the list rooted at every node, as with subtree-of-another-tree
items.answer: Find the middle with slow/fast pointers, reverse the second half, then walk both halves comparing values
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: a linked list has no tree shape or ordering invariant — palindrome-checking here relies on linked-list-specific techniques (fast/slow pointers, in-place reversal), not tree recursion.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether every opening bracket in a string has a matching, correctly-nested closing bracket. What's the best approach?
options: Push opening brackets onto a stack, and pop-and-compare whenever a closing bracket is seen
Recurse comparing the string's first half against its second half node by node
Treat the string as two 'trees' (before and after the midpoint) and run a same-tree check
BFS through the string's characters level by level
items.answer: Push opening brackets onto a stack, and pop-and-compare whenever a closing bracket is seen
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: nested bracket matching is the canonical stack (LIFO) problem — there's no tree here to recurse over or compare structurally.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether one binary tree is a mirror of a SECOND, separate binary tree (not itself) — same shape, but flipped left-right. What's the best approach?
options: Recurse on both trees in lockstep, but cross-wise: the first tree's left against the second tree's right, and vice versa
Flatten both trees with pre-order traversal and check if one list equals the reverse of the other
BFS both trees separately and compare their level-by-level node counts
Hash each tree's multiset of values and compare the two hashes
items.answer: Recurse on both trees in lockstep, but cross-wise: the first tree's left against the second tree's right, and vice versa
items.explanation_md: Mirroring across two separate trees is the same cross-wise structural recursion as checking a single tree's self-symmetry, just anchored at two different roots instead of one.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the maximum depth (height) of a binary tree. What's the best approach?
options: DFS returning 1 + max(left subtree depth, right subtree depth) at each node
Recurse comparing the tree against itself node by node, as in a same-tree check
Walk the tree using BST ordering to prune to a single path
Two pointers converging from the tree's shallowest and deepest leaves
items.answer: DFS returning 1 + max(left subtree depth, right subtree depth) at each node
items.explanation_md: Maximum depth is a bottom-up subtree aggregate computed with a single DFS pass — distinct from the two-tree comparison shape this lesson focuses on, but still a tree-recursion technique, not a two-tree matcher.
